label: ai
topic: A father with Parkinson's collapses the night of a rocket launch
text:
The night smells like damp grass and something faintly metallic, like rain that hasn't quite arrived. You sit on the porch beside your father, the air warm enough that you don't need a jacket. Somewhere in the distance, a rocket is preparing to launch, though you won't see it from here—only the delayed glow, the echo of something leaving.

He leans back in his chair, hands resting in his lap. They tremble, subtly, a rhythm you've learned not to stare at. "When I was a kid," he says, "I thought space was the quietest place imaginable." His voice is steady, thoughtful. "But it's not, really. Not the way we think of quiet."

You nod, though you're not sure what he means. The porch light hums softly above you.

Inside, the house carries the weight of recent weeks—hospital discharge papers on the counter, pill bottles lined up with careful precision, the faint antiseptic smell that clings to everything. His third spinal surgery. The word "third" sits heavily, as if counting makes it more real.

But out here, you talk about space.

At 9:12, he points upward, though the sky is mostly empty. "It's all movement," he says. "Even when it looks still." You follow his gaze, trying to imagine it—the constant motion, the invisible forces. You think about how bodies move, too, how they change in ways you can't always control.

He tells you about rockets, about the physics of escape velocity, about the way something has to reach a certain speed to break free from Earth's pull. You listen, letting the words settle. You don't mention gravity in any other context.

At 9:26, there's a faint glow on the horizon. It's brief, almost easy to miss. He smiles when he sees it. "There," he says, as if confirming something.

For a moment, it feels like a shared victory.

Later, inside, the kitchen light is too bright. He sits at the table, a glass of water in front of him. You move around him, rinsing dishes, wiping down counters that don't need wiping. The ordinary choreography of being in a room together.

At 10:03, he says your name. You turn. There's something in his voice you can't quite place.

At 10:04, the glass tips, water spilling across the table. His hand doesn't catch it.

At 10:05, everything shifts. The chair scrapes against the floor as you move toward him. His body slumps, the careful balance of it undone. You say his name now, louder, as if volume might anchor him.

Time doesn't stop. It stretches.

You think of the porch, of the sky, of the way he described movement—constant, invisible, unstoppable. You think of rockets, of the precise moment they break free.

At 10:07, you are holding his shoulders, trying to keep him upright, as if that might be enough.

The house is no longer quiet. Or maybe it never was.
